chunks.title: College of Engineering Computer Science and Engineering CSCE 612 Syllabus
chunks.content:
College of Engineering
Computer Science and Engineering
CSCE 612 Syllabus
Section 600 (42640)
Applied Networks and Distributed Processing
Spring 2026 - College Station

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Applied Networks and Distributed Processing. (3-0). Credit 3. Fundamentals, including network design and protocol analysis, in the context of computer communications; mixes fundamentals with both programming and pragmatic views of engineering issues; it includes network architecture as well as principles of network engineering; focus is on applying principles of layered architecture to analyzing real networks; lab exercises focus on protocol understanding and programming; knowledge of UNIX and C programming helpful, but not required. Only one of the following will satisfy the requirements for a degree: CSCE 463 or CSCE 612. Prerequisite: Graduate classification.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content: Because this is a stacked course with CSCE 463, graduate students should look for 612-specific sections in each assignment for additional requirements and learning opportunities.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content: At the end of the semester, the students will be able to utilize operating-system sockets APIs, create efficient network applications and build multi-threaded decentralized frameworks, analyze performance and security of networks, develop protocols, and construct models of their system.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Computer Networking
ISBN: 978-0132856201
Authors: James F. Kurose, Keith W. Ross
Publication Date: 2013-01-01
Edition: 6th

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
A: 90-100%, B: 80-89%, C: 70-79%, D: 60-69%, F: 0-59%

Grade distribution
| Assignment | Qty | Format | Percent of final grade |
|:-----------|:----|:-------|:-----------------------|
| Homework   | 4   | Implementation &report | 40% (10% each)         |
| Midterms   | 3   | Closed-book | 45% (15% each)         |
| Quizzes    | 3   | Closed-book | 15% (5% each)          |

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Homework submissions are due at 10 am. Late homework is acceptable with a penalty of 20% of the full grade per day (no points after 5 days). Delays are rounded up to the nearest day. Example #1: your homework scores 76 points out of 100, but is 28 hours late. Your score is then reduced by 40 points and becomes 36. Example #2: your part1 submission scores 19 out of 25 pts, but is 1 day late. The reduction is then 5 pts, which brings the grade down to 14.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
The following schedule will be in effect (* marks assignment due dates):

Schedule
| Lecture # | Title | Topic |
|:----------|:------|:------|
| 1         | Preliminaries 1 | Syllabus, Visual Studio |
| 2         | Preliminaries 2 | Sockets, threads, synchronization |
| 3         | Introduction 1* | Internet structure |
| 4         | Introduction 2 | Topology, delay, loss |
| 5         | Application layer 1* | Application types, HTTP |
| 6         | Quiz 1 | Chapter 1 |
| 7         | Application layer 2 | Caching, cookies, FTP, email |
| 8         | Application layer 3 | DNS basics |
| 9         | Application layer 4* | DNS vulnerabilities |
| 10        | Midterm 1 | Chapters 1-2, homework #1 |
| 11        | Application layer 5 | Other DNS uses, P2P |
| 12        | Transport layer 1 | Overview, multiplexing, UDP |
| 13        | Transport Layer 2 | Reliable data transfer, stop & wait |
| 14        | Quiz 2 | Chapter 2, parts of 3 |
| 15        | Transport Layer 3* | Go-back-N, Selective Repeat |
| 16        | Transport Layer 4 | Timeouts, flow control, fast retx |
| 17        | Transport Layer 5 | Congestion control |
| 18        | Midterm 2 | Chapter 3, homework #2 |
| 19        | Transport Layer 6* | TCP modeling, fairness |
| 20        | Network Layer 1 | Introduction |
| 21        | Network Layer 2 | Switching, IP header |
| 22        | Quiz 3 | Chapter 4 |
| 23        | Network Layer 3* | NAT, link-state routing |
| 24        | Network Layer 4 | Distance vector, RIP, OSPF |
| 25        | Network Layer 5 | BGP, multicast |
| 26        | Data-link Layer 1* | Error detection/correction, CSMA |
| 27        | Data-link Layer 2 | Ethernet, hubs/switches |
| 28        | Midterm 3 | Chapters 4-5, homework #3-4 |

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
All homework submissions must compile in Visual Studio 2022 in both debug and release mode. Solutions with missing files, incorrect project options, or dependency on non-standard libraries (e.g., boost) will not be graded.
Each homework must be accompanied by a written report (if there are multiple parts in the homework, the report applies only to the last part) describing your implementation and showing the performance analysis requested in the handout. Write in as much detail as possible, explain the various observations, and comment on the sanity of obtained results.

chunks.title: Academic Dishonesty Policy (Course Specific)
chunks.content: This course assumes independent work on each assignment. All submissions must be original and developed from scratch. At no time can you receive aid from external sources (e.g., current students, prior students, material found online, Al tools). The only exceptions to this policy are: sample code provided by the instructor on the course website, Microsoft help pages, and any interaction with the TA/instructor. Academic dishonesty, including cheating and plagiarism, will result in an F* for the course and may lead to expulsion from the university.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: Any usage of AI, copilot, or LLMs is prohibited. Violations will be treated as cheating and will result in an F*.
